title: P/E Ratio and EPS — The Core Valuation Metrics
objectives: Calculate earnings per share (EPS) from a company's financial data
Calculate and interpret the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio
Compare two companies using P/E and explain what a difference in P/E implies

vocabulary.definition: Net profit after tax divided by number of shares on issue. The profit each share earns. Higher EPS generally means a more profitable company per share.
Share price divided by EPS. Shows how many years of current earnings investors are paying. A P/E of 20 means investors pay 20 years of current earnings for one share.
P/E calculated using forecast future earnings rather than historical earnings. More forward-looking but relies on earnings estimates that may prove wrong.
The percentage increase in a company's EPS from one year to the next. Growing earnings typically justify a higher P/E ratio.
P/E divided by earnings growth rate. A PEG of 1.0 is considered fair value — accounts for the fact that faster-growing companies deserve higher P/E ratios.

activities.title: Earnings Per Share — How Much Profit Per Share?
activities.notePrompt: Calculate EPS for: Company A earns $45 million profit with 150 million shares. Company B earns $120 million profit with 500 million shares. Which has higher EPS? Which is more profitable in absolute terms?
activities.content:
Earnings per share (EPS) is the most fundamental measure of a company's profitability on a per-share basis.

Formula: EPS = Net profit after tax ÷ Shares on issue

Example — Fisher and Paykel Healthcare:
Net profit 2024: $310 million
Shares on issue: 579 million
EPS = $310,000,000 ÷ 579,000,000 = $0.535 per share

EPS tells you: each FPH share earned $0.535 of profit in 2024.

Why EPS matters more than total profit: A company with $500 million profit and 2 billion shares ($0.25 EPS) is less profitable per share than a company with $100 million profit and 150 million shares ($0.67 EPS). EPS normalises profit by the number of shares, allowing fair comparison between companies of different sizes.

EPS growth: The change in EPS from year to year is even more important than the absolute level. If FPH's EPS was $0.45 last year and is $0.535 this year, EPS grew 18.9% — a strong signal of improving business performance.

Diluted EPS: Companies also report diluted EPS, which accounts for shares that might be issued in future (from employee options, convertible bonds etc.). Diluted EPS is always equal to or lower than basic EPS, and is the more conservative measure.

activities.title: The P/E Ratio — How Much Are You Paying for Profits?
activities.notePrompt: If Mainfreight has an EPS of $4.20 and a share price of $70, what is its P/E? If the NZX average P/E is 22×, is Mainfreight cheap or expensive? What might justify the difference?
activities.content:
The price-to-earnings ratio is the most widely used valuation metric in investing.

Formula: P/E = Share price ÷ EPS

Example:
Mainfreight share price: $70.00
Mainfreight EPS: $4.20
P/E = $70 ÷ $4.20 = 16.7×

What P/E means: You are paying $16.70 for every $1 of annual earnings. Or equivalently: if earnings stay constant, it would take 16.7 years of earnings to pay back your purchase price.

How to interpret P/E:
— NZX 50 average P/E: approximately 20× to 25×
— Low P/E (below 15×): potentially cheap, or market expects earnings to decline
— High P/E (above 30×): potentially expensive, or market expects strong future earnings growth
— Very high P/E (above 50×): market is betting on dramatic future growth — very common in tech companies

Context matters: Comparing P/E only makes sense within the same industry or against the historical average for that company. A 25× P/E is cheap for a fast-growing software company but expensive for a slow-growth utility.

NZ examples (approximate 2024 P/E):
Fisher and Paykel Healthcare: ~38× (growth company premium)
Mainfreight: ~17× (reasonable for a quality logistics company)
Spark NZ: ~19× (mature, steady telco)
Meridian Energy: ~30× (renewable energy premium)

activities.statement: A low P/E ratio always means a share is cheap and represents a good buying opportunity.
activities.explanation: False. A low P/E can mean a share is cheap — or it can mean the market expects the company's earnings to decline, making today's EPS a misleading starting point. This is called a 'value trap': a company looks cheap on P/E but earnings fall, and the P/E was low because the market correctly anticipated the decline. Always ask WHY a P/E is low before concluding it is a bargain. Is it cheap because it is overlooked, or cheap because the business is deteriorating?

activities.title: Comparing Companies Using P/E — Worked Example
activities.notePrompt: Using the Spark vs Contact Energy comparison, write two sentences: one explaining why Contact Energy might deserve a higher P/E, one explaining why a higher P/E might be unjustified.
activities.content:
P/E comparison between two NZ energy companies:

Spark NZ (SPK): Telecommunications
Share price: $4.20 | EPS: $0.22 | P/E: 19.1×
Growth rate: ~3% per year | Dividend yield: 6.5%

Contact Energy (CEN): Electricity generation and retail
Share price: $8.35 | EPS: $0.36 | P/E: 23.2×

Growth rate: ~6% per year | Dividend yield: 4.6%

Contact Energy trades at a higher P/E (23.2×) than Spark (19.1×). Why might this be justified?
— Contact Energy is growing earnings faster (6% vs 3%) — higher growth justifies higher multiple
— Renewable electricity is benefiting from electrification trends (EVs, heat pumps) — structural tailwind
— Investors may view electricity as having better long-term demand growth than telecom

Why the higher P/E might NOT be justified:
— Spark's dividend yield (6.5%) is significantly higher than Contact's (4.6%)
— Telecom is a more predictable, stable business with less risk
— If Contact's earnings growth slows, the 23.2× P/E looks expensive

The PEG ratio helps adjust for growth:
Spark PEG: 19.1 ÷ 3 = 6.4 (very high — paying a lot per unit of growth)
Contact PEG: 23.2 ÷ 6 = 3.9 (still high but less so given faster growth)

Neither is 'cheap' on PEG but Contact Energy offers better value on a growth-adjusted basis.

questions.question: Meridian Energy earns $320 million net profit and has 1,600 million shares on issue. What is its EPS?
options: $2.00 — net profit in cents per share
$320 — total profit not adjusted for share count
questions.explanation: $320,000,000 ÷ 1,600,000,000 = $0.20 EPS. Each Meridian share earned $0.20 of profit in the year. At a share price of $5.60, the P/E ratio would be $5.60 ÷ $0.20 = 28×. This means investors are paying 28 years of current earnings for each share — reflecting Meridian's position as a high-quality renewable energy company with strong long-term demand growth prospects.

questions.question: Company A has a P/E of 12× and Company B has a P/E of 35×. Company A is a mature grocery retailer growing at 2% per year. Company B is a healthcare technology firm growing at 25% per year. Which valuation is likely more reasonable?
options: Company A is clearly the better buy — a P/E of 12 is always cheaper and safer than 35
Company B is overvalued at 35× — no company is worth more than 20× earnings
Both valuations may be reasonable given their growth profiles — 12× for a 2% grower and 35× for a 25% grower can both represent fair value depending on the quality and sustainability of the growth
Company A is overvalued at 12× because grocery retail is a declining industry
questions.explanation: P/E must be evaluated relative to growth. A 2% growing grocery retailer trading at 12× P/E is actually quite richly valued — PEG = 12÷2 = 6×. A 25% growing healthcare tech at 35× has PEG = 35÷25 = 1.4× — actually better value on a growth-adjusted basis. Both could be reasonably valued. P/E in isolation tells you nothing without growth context.

questions.question: Fisher and Paykel Healthcare's EPS was $0.45 last year and is $0.535 this year. What is the EPS growth rate?
options: 0.085% — the raw difference between the two EPS figures
8.5% — (0.535 − 0.45) ÷ 0.535 × 100 using the new EPS as base
53.5% — this year's EPS as a percentage of one dollar
questions.explanation: EPS growth = (new EPS − old EPS) ÷ old EPS × 100 = ($0.535 − $0.45) ÷ $0.45 × 100 = $0.085 ÷ $0.45 × 100 = 18.9%. Always divide by the starting (old) value when calculating percentage growth. An 18.9% EPS growth rate is very strong and typically supports a premium P/E multiple.

activities.title: High P/E or Low P/E? Match the Scenario

pairs.item: Fast-growing NZ tech company with 40% annual earnings growth
Mature NZ electricity retailer with 3% annual growth and high dividend
Early-stage biotech with no profits yet but promising drug pipeline
Established NZ logistics company with consistent 8% earnings growth
Company whose earnings have fallen 60% — market expects further decline
pairs.zone: Misleadingly High P/E — earnings may fall further

activities.explanation: P/E reflects both current earnings and the market's expectations for future earnings growth. High-growth companies deserve high P/E multiples. Stable, low-growth businesses trade at lower multiples. Unprofitable companies have no meaningful P/E. And companies with collapsing earnings can show misleadingly high P/E — the number looks high but it is because earnings have plunged, not because the company is valued generously.

activities.statement: The PEG ratio adjusts the P/E ratio for earnings growth, making it more useful for comparing a fast-growing company to a slow-growing one.
activities.explanation: True. PEG (P/E ÷ earnings growth rate) accounts for the fact that faster-growing companies deserve higher P/E multiples. A company with a 30× P/E and 30% growth has PEG = 1.0 — considered fair value. A company with 30× P/E and 3% growth has PEG = 10 — considered very expensive. PEG = 1.0 is traditionally the dividing line between cheap (below 1) and expensive (above 1), though like all metrics it is a guide not a rule.

activities.question: A company has a share price of $15.60 and EPS of $0.78. What is its P/E ratio?
activities.explanation: $15.60 ÷ $0.78 = 20. The P/E ratio is 20×. This means investors are paying $20 for every $1 of annual earnings. Compared to the NZX 50 average of approximately 20–25×, this is at the low end of market average — suggesting either fair value or that the market expects low future earnings growth.

activities.title: Find a Real P/E
activities.prompt: Look up any NZ company on nzx.com or Google Finance. Find its P/E ratio. Find its sector average P/E (you may need to compare to similar companies). Write: Is your company above or below its sector average P/E? What might explain this — is the company growing faster, has it had a bad year, or is it genuinely better or worse quality than peers?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — interpreting P/E in context is one of the most useful practical investment skills.
